title: Coarctation of the Aorta
chief_complaint: Newborn presents in cardiogenic shock at 2 weeks of age. Poor feeding, lethargic, gray-colored, cold extremities, barely palpable pulses, difficulty breathing.

physical_exam.General: Lethargic, mottled, cold extremities, weak cry
physical_exam.Cardiac: Tachycardia, gallop rhythm (S3). Nonspecific systolic murmur. RV heave.
physical_exam.Pulses: Upper extremity: Weak but palpable. Lower extremity: ABSENT femoral pulses. Radio-femoral delay.
physical_exam.Hepatomegaly: Liver 4cm below costal margin (congestive heart failure)

hidden_data.cxr: Cardiomegaly. Pulmonary edema. Rib notching NOT present (develops after 6 years - collateral circulation).
hidden_data.echo: Coarctation of aorta (juxtaductal - at insertion of ductus arteriosus). Severe narrowing (3mm). Bicuspid aortic valve (present in 50-85% of coarctation). LV dysfunction (EF 30%). Diastolic flow continuation in descending aorta on pulse wave Doppler (pathognomonic).
hidden_data.abg: pH 7.05, PaCO2 55, PaO2 50, Lactate 12, HCO3 10 (severe metabolic acidosis - cardiogenic shock)
hidden_data.cmp: Creatinine 2.0 (AKI from hypoperfusion), BUN 45, AST 300 (shock liver)
hidden_data.genetic: Turner syndrome ruled out (normal male karyotype 46,XY)

correct_diagnosis: Critical Coarctation of the Aorta (Juxtaductal) presenting with Cardiogenic Shock when Ductus Arteriosus Closed
key_learning_points: Coarctation = narrowing of aorta, usually juxtaductal (at ductus arteriosus insertion). More common in males, associated with bicuspid aortic valve (50-85%), Turner syndrome (30% have coarctation)
Ductal-dependent lesion: When PDA closes (day 2-14 of life), sudden increase in afterload → severe LV failure → cardiogenic shock. Collateral circulation not yet developed in neonates
Classic finding: Upper extremity hypertension + Lower extremity hypotension + Absent/delayed femoral pulses. BP differential >20mmHg is diagnostic
CXR findings: Rib notching (from collateral intercostal arteries) develops after age 6-8 years. Figure-3 sign (aortic knob + post-stenotic dilation + coarcted segment)

Treatment: Prostaglandin E1 (PGE1) to reopen ductus arteriosus - LIFE-SAVING in neonates presenting in shock. Surgical repair (resection + end-to-end anastomosis) or balloon angioplasty with stent. Timing: Neonates with CHF = urgent repair. Stable older children = elective
